title: Reverse Bits
description:
Given an integer `n`, treat it as a 32-bit unsigned integer (pad with leading zeros as needed) and return the integer formed by reversing the order of its 32 bits.

**Example**

```
n = 43261596   (00000010100101000001111010011100)  ->  964176192   (00111001011110000010100101000000)
n = 1                                                ->  2147483648
```
starter_code:
def reverse_bits(n):
    # n: int, treated as a 32-bit unsigned integer. Return n with its 32 bits
    # reversed, as an integer.
    pass
hints: Build the result one bit at a time: shift the result left, OR in the lowest bit of n, then shift n right, for 32 iterations.

Python ints are unbounded (no fixed 32-bit width), so nothing wraps automatically — just make sure the final result only ever has bits set within the 32-bit range, which the shift-and-OR construction guarantees.

solutions.name: Bit-by-bit shift and OR
solutions.explanation: Process n's 32 bits one at a time from least significant to most significant. On each of 32 iterations, shift the accumulated result left by one to make room, OR in n's current lowest bit (n & 1), then shift n right to expose the next bit. After 32 iterations the bits have been placed in reverse order, producing the reversed 32-bit value directly (Python's unbounded ints don't need explicit masking here since only 32 bits are ever written into the result).
